old.claim_text: The Hasselblad camera used during the moonwalk was thought to be lost or left on the Moon surface.
old.subject: The Hasselblad camera

new.claim_text: The Hasselblad camera used during the moonwalk was thought to be lost or left on the Moon's surface.
new.subject: The Hasselblad camera

old.claim_text: The aircraft carrier , under the command of Captain Carl J. Seiberlich, was selected as the primary recovery ship (PRS) for Apollo 11 on June 5, replacing its sister ship, the LPH , which had recovered Apollo 10 on May 26.
old.subject: The aircraft carrier

new.claim_text: The aircraft carrier , under the command of Captain Carl J. Seiberlich, was selected as the primary recovery ship (PRS) for Apollo 11 on June 5, replacing her sister ship, the LPH , which had recovered Apollo 10 on May 26.
new.subject: The aircraft carrier

old.claim_text: When Armstrong again looked outside, he saw that the computer's landing target was in a boulder-strewn area just north and east of a crater (later determined to be West crater), so he took semi-automatic control.
old.subject: he

new.claim_text: When Armstrong again looked outside, he saw that the computer's landing system was targeting a boulder-strewn area just north and east of a crater (later determined to be West crater), so he took semi-automatic control.
new.subject: he

old.claim_text: Doctors diagnosed the problem as a bony growth between his fifth and sixth vertebrae, requiring surgery.
old.subject: Doctors

new.claim_text: Doctors diagnosed a bony growth between his fifth and sixth vertebrae, requiring surgery.
new.subject: Doctors

old.claim_text: President Dwight D. Eisenhower responded to the Sputnik challenge by creating the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), and initiating Project Mercury, which aimed to launch a man into Earth orbit.
old.subject: President Dwight D. Eisenhower

new.claim_text: President Dwight D. Eisenhower responded to the challenge posed by Sputnik by creating the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), and initiating Project Mercury, which aimed to place a man into Earth orbit.
new.subject: President Dwight D. Eisenhower

old.claim_text:
(left), with backup crewmen Jim Lovell and Fred Haise listening in during Apollo 11's descent]]

The capsule communicator (CAPCOM) was an astronaut at the Mission Control Center in Houston, Texas, who was the only person who communicated directly with the flight crew.
old.subject: The capsule communicator

new.claim_text:
(left), with backup crewmembers Lovell and Haise listening in during Apollo 11's descent]]

The capsule communicator (CAPCOM) was an astronaut stationed at the Mission Control Center in Houston, Texas, and was typically the only person permitted to communicate directly with the flight crew.
new.subject: The capsule communicator

old.claim_text: On October 4, 1957, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite.
old.subject: the Soviet Union

new.claim_text: On October 4, 1957, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite, surprising the world and fueling fears about Soviet technological and military capabilities.
new.subject: the Soviet Union

old.claim_text: For Apollo 11, the support crew consisted of Ken Mattingly, Ronald Evans and Bill Pogue.
old.subject: the support crew

new.claim_text: The support crew for Apollo 11 was Mattingly, Ronald Evans, and Bill Pogue.
new.subject: The support crew

old.claim_text: By the normal crew rotation in place during Apollo, Lovell, Mattingly, and Haise were scheduled to fly on Apollo 14, but the three of them were bumped to Apollo 13: there was a crew issue for Apollo 13 as none of them except Edgar Mitchell flew in space again.
old.subject: Haise

new.claim_text: Under the normal Apollo crew rotation, Lovell, Mattingly, and Haise would have been assigned to Apollo 14.
new.subject: Haise

added.claim_text: Its success demonstrated that the USSR could potentially deliver nuclear weapons over intercontinental distances, challenging American claims of military, economic, and technological superiority.
added.subject: Its success

added.claim_text: This incident sparked the Sputnik crisis and ignited the Space Race, as both superpowers sought to demonstrate superiority in spaceflight.
added.subject: This incident

added.claim_text: The Soviets took the lead on April 12, 1961, when cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin became the first person in space and the first to orbit the Earth.
added.subject: The Soviets

added.claim_text: Nearly a month later, on May 5, 1961, Alan Shepard became the first American in space; his 15-minute flight was suborbital, not a full orbit.
added.subject: his 15-minute flight

added.claim_text: Because the Soviet Union had launch vehicles with higher lift capacity, President John F. Kennedy, Eisenhower's successor, chose a challenge that exceeded the capabilities of the existing generation of rockets, so that both countries would be starting from an equal position.
added.subject: President John F. Kennedy

added.claim_text: However, because Alan Shepard had only recently returned to flight status and needed additional training, NASA reassigned his crew to Apollo 14 and moved Lovell's crew up to fly Apollo 13 instead.
added.subject: NASA

added.claim_text: Shortly before launch, Mattingly was replaced by Jack Swigert due to concerns over possible measles exposure.
added.subject: Mattingly

added.claim_text: For Apollo, NASA added a third group, the support crew.
added.subject: NASA

added.claim_text: Their job was to maintain the flight plan, checklists, and mission rules, and to keep the prime and backup crews updated on any changes.
added.subject: Their job

added.claim_text: They also developed procedures—especially for emergencies, so that by the time the prime and backup crews entered the simulators, they could focus on practicing and mastering them.
added.subject: They

added.claim_text: This practice ensured that messages came from someone best able to understand the situation in the spacecraft and to relay guidance from flight controllers as clearly as possible.
added.subject: This practice

added.claim_text: For Apollo 11, the CAPCOMs were backup and support crew members Anders, Evans, Haise, Lovell, and Mattingly, along with Charles Duke, Owen Garriott, Don L. Lind, Bruce McCandless II, and Harrison Schmitt.
added.subject: the CAPCOMs

added.claim_text: The remote-control unit on his chest kept him from seeing his feet.
added.subject: The remote-control unit

added.claim_text: Following their return to Earth, the Apollo 11 crew received widespread international acclaim.
added.subject: the Apollo 11 crew

added.claim_text: On August 13, 1969, astronauts Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins were honored with ticker-tape parades in New York City and Chicago, with an estimated six million people lining the streets.
added.subject: astronauts Neil Armstrong

added.claim_text: That evening, a state dinner was held in Los Angeles at the Century Plaza Hotel to commemorate the historic achievement.
added.subject: a state dinner

qualifiers.spatial: Los Angeles, the Century Plaza Hotel

added.claim_text: The event was attended by members of Congress, 44 state governors, Chief Justice of the United States Warren E. Burger and his predecessor Earl Warren, as well as ambassadors from 83 nations.
added.subject: The event

added.claim_text: President Richard Nixon and Vice President Spiro Agnew awarded each astronaut the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the highest civilian honor in the United States.
added.subject: President Richard Nixon

added.claim_text: On September 16, the astronauts addressed a joint session of Congress, where they presented two American flags—one to the House of Representatives and one to the Senate—that had traveled to the lunar surface aboard Eagle.
added.subject: REF

added.claim_text: One of the mission flags carried aboard the flight—the Flag of American Samoa—was later displayed at the Jean P. Haydon Museum in Pago Pago, the capital of American Samoa, recognizing the territory’s support role as a recovery staging area.
added.subject: REF

added.claim_text: The celebrations continued with a 38-day world goodwill tour titled "Giant Leap", which began on September 29 and concluded on November 5, 1969.
added.subject: The celebrations

added.claim_text: The astronauts visited 22 countries and met with numerous heads of state, prime ministers, royalty, and civic leaders.
added.subject: The astronauts

added.claim_text: The tour, intended to thank the international community for their support of the space program, began in Mexico City and ended in Tokyo.
added.subject: The tour

added.claim_text: Notable stops included Bogotá, Buenos Aires, Rio de Janeiro, Madrid, Paris, Amsterdam, Brussels, London, Rome, Belgrade, Tehran, Mumbai, Bangkok, Sydney, Guam, and Honolulu.
added.subject: Notable stops

qualifiers.spatial: Rio de Janeiro, Madrid, Paris, Amsterdam, Brussels, London, Rome, Belgrade, Tehran, Mumbai, Bangkok, Sydney, Guam, Honolulu

added.claim_text: Crowds in the tens or hundreds of thousands gathered to greet the astronauts in each city.
added.subject: REF:tour-itinerary] Crowds

added.claim_text: Numerous countries and organizations honored the Moon landing by issuing special commemorative items.
added.subject: Numerous countries

added.claim_text: These included postage stamps, coins, medals, plaques, and magazine features.
added.subject: These

added.claim_text: TIME, National Geographic, LIFE, and dozens of international publications featured the astronauts on their covers.
added.subject: TIME

added.claim_text: Many of these commemoratives are now held in public and private collections, and some were placed aboard later Apollo missions in symbolic tribute.
added.subject: Many

added.claim_text: Additionally, the success of Apollo 11 contributed to a brief spike in interest in science and technology education, often referred to as the “Apollo effect,” influencing a generation of engineers and scientists.
added.subject: REF

# Liftoff at 09:32 EST (13:32 UTC) from Kennedy Space Center LC-39A

# Trans-lunar injection burn began at ~12:22 EST
# Escaped Earth's gravitational influence at ~16:16 EST
#

Carried goodwill disc from 73 nations and Apollo 1 tribute

# Continued communication via Deep Space Network

Mission Day 3 (Final System Checks) Summary

# Lunar Module Eagle inspected through tunnel
# Second television broadcast
# Armstrong:

Earth is a “beautiful oasis in space”

Summary

# lunar orbits completed
# Sea of Tranquility confirmed as landing site

# Lunar Module (LM) Eagle undocked; descent initiated; 1202 program alarm resolved
# LANDED
added.subject: 1202 program alarm

added.claim_text:
AT 16:17 EST – “THE EAGLE HAS LANDED”
# First extravehicular activity (EVA): Armstrong and Aldrin began moonwalk at 22:56 EST
# "That's one small step..." / "Magnificent desolation"
# U.S. flag planted, experiments deployed
# 21.5 kg of lunar material collected
# Broadcast watched live by an estimated 600 million people worldwide, one of the most significant television events of the 20th century

Mission Day 6 (Lunar Liftoff and Docking)

16:17 EST, 22:56 EST, the 20th century

Mission Day 6

added.claim_text:
Summary

# Lunar module (LM) ascent from Moon at 13:54 EST
# Docking with Command Module successful
# LM jettisoned into lunar orbit (final crash site unknown)
Mission Day 7 (Begin Return to Earth)

Summary

# Trans-Earth injection burn initiated
# Routine television and radio updates continued
# Crew demonstrated weightlessness
# Lunar dust noted to smell like “wet ashes”

Mission Day 8 (Final Space Day)
added.subject:
Summary

# Trans-Earth injection burn

Summary

# Final television broadcast: reflections and gratitude shared at 21:17 EST
# Crew donned biological isolation garments
# Final reentry checks and orientation

# Splashdown at 12:50 EST in the Pacific Ocean
# Recovery by USS Hornet
# Crew entered Mobile Quarantine Facility (MQF); greeted by President Nixon
# Total mission duration: 8 days, 3 hours, 18 minutes
added.subject: reflections
